{"result":"     1	"""
     2	Configuration file parser.
     3	Supports nested sections, key=value pairs, includes, and variable interpolation.
     4	"""
     5	
     6	import re
     7	from pathlib import Path
     8	
     9	
    10	class ParseError(Exception):
    11	    def __init__(self, message, line_num=None, file=None):
    12	        self.line_num = line_num
    13	        self.file = file
    14	        super().__init__(f"{file}:{line_num}: {message}" if line_num else message)
    15	
    16	
    17	class ConfigParser:
    18	    def __init__(self, base_path="."):
    19	        self.base_path = Path(base_path)
    20	        self.variables = {}
    21	        self._include_stack = []
    22	
    23	    def parse_file(self, path):
    24	        """Parse a config file and return a nested dict."""
    25	        resolved = self.base_path / path
    26	        if resolved in self._include_stack:
    27	            raise ParseError(f"Circular include: {path}", file=str(resolved))
    28	        self._include_stack.append(resolved)
    29	        try:
    30	            text = resolved.read_text()
    31	            result = self._parse_text(text, str(resolved))
    32	            return result
    33	        finally:
    34	            self._include_stack.pop()
    35	
    36	    def _parse_text(self, text, filename="<string>"):
    37	        """Parse config text into a nested dict."""
    38	        root = {}
    39	        stack = [root]
    40	        current = root
    41	
    42	        for line_num, raw_line in enumerate(text.splitlines(), 1):
    43	            line = raw_line.strip()
    44	
    45	            # Skip empty lines and comments
    46	            if not line or line.startswith("#"):
    47	                continue
    48	
    49	            # Include directive
    50	            if line.startswith("@include"):
    51	                include_path = line[len("@include"):].strip().strip('"\'')
    52	                included = self.parse_file(include_path)
    53	                current.update(included)
    54	                continue
    55	
    56	            # Variable definition: $var = value
    57	            if line.startswith("$"):
    58	                match = re.match(r'\$(\w+)\s*=\s*(.*)', line)
    59	                if match:
    60	                    self.variables[match.group(1)] = match.group(2)
    61	                    continue
    62	                raise ParseError(f"Invalid variable definition: {line}", line_num, filename)
    63	
    64	            # Section start: [section.subsection]
    65	            if line.startswith("[") and line.endswith("]"):
    66	                section_path = line[1:-1].strip()
    67	                parts = section_path.split(".")
    68	
    69	                # Navigate to parent, creating intermediate sections
    70	                target = root
    71	                for part in parts:
    72	                    if part not in target:
    73	                        target[part] = {}
    74	                    elif not isinstance(target[part], dict):
    75	                        raise ParseError(
    76	                            f"Cannot create section '{section_path}': "
    77	                            f"'{part}' already exists as a value",
    78	                            line_num, filename
    79	                        )
    80	                    target = target[part]
    81	
    82	                # BUG: stack is not updated correctly for nested sections.
    83	                # When we see [a.b], we should set current = target (which is root[a][b]).
    84	                # But the stack should also be updated so that closing a section
    85	                # returns to the right level. Currently we just append, so
    86	                # [a] then [a.b] then [c] leaves current pointing at root[c]
    87	                # but stack still has the old entries.
    88	                stack = [root]
    89	                current = target
    90	                continue
    91	
    92	            # Key = value
    93	            if "=" in line:
    94	                key, _, value = line.partition("=")
    95	                key = key.strip()
    96	                value = value.strip()
    97	
    98	                # Strip quotes
    99	                if (value.startswith('"') and value.endswith('"')) or \
   100	                   (value.startswith("'") and value.endswith("'")):
   101	                    value = value[1:-1]
   102	
   103	                # Variable interpolation: ${var}
   104	                value = self._interpolate(value, line_num, filename)
   105	
   106	                # Type coercion
   107	                value = self._coerce(value)
   108	
   109	                current[key] = value
   110	                continue
   111	
   112	            raise ParseError(f"Unrecognized syntax: {line}", line_num, filename)
   113	
   114	        return root
   115	
   116	    def _interpolate(self, value, line_num, filename):
   117	        """Replace ${var} references with variable values."""
   118	        def replacer(match):
   119	            var_name = match.group(1)
   120	            if var_name in self.variables:
   121	                return self.variables[var_name]
   122	            # BUG: silently returns empty string instead of raising an error
   123	            # for undefined variables. This causes subtle data loss.
   124	            return ""
   125	
   126	        return re.sub(r'\$\{(\w+)\}', replacer, value)
   127	
   128	    def _coerce(self, value):
   129	        """Coerce string values to appropriate Python types."""
   130	        if value.lower() in ("true", "yes", "on"):
   131	            return True
   132	        if value.lower() in ("false", "no", "off"):
   133	            return False
   134	        if value.lower() in ("null", "none", ""):
   135	            return None
   136	        try:
   137	            return int(value)
   138	        except ValueError:
   139	            pass
   140	        try:
   141	            return float(value)
   142	        except ValueError:
   143	            pass
   144	        # BUG: values that look like lists (comma-separated) are not parsed.
   145	        # "a, b, c" stays as the string "a, b, c" instead of becoming ["a", "b", "c"].
   146	        # The test expects list parsing but it's not implemented.
   147	        return value
"}

1	"""
     2	Configuration file parser.
     3	Supports nested sections, key=value pairs, includes, and variable interpolation.
     4	"""
     5	
     6	import re
     7	from pathlib import Path
     8	
     9	
    10	class ParseError(Exception):
    11	    def __init__(self, message, line_num=None, file=None):
    12	        self.line_num = line_num
    13	        self.file = file
    14	        super().__init__(f"{file}:{line_num}: {message}" if line_num else message)
    15	
    16	
    17	class ConfigParser:
    18	    def __init__(self, base_path="."):
    19	        self.base_path = Path(base_path)
    20	        self.variables = {}
    21	        self._include_stack = []
    22	
    23	    def parse_file(self, path):
    24	        """Parse a config file and return a nested dict."""
    25	        resolved = self.base_path / path
    26	        if resolved in self._include_stack:
    27	            raise ParseError(f"Circular include: {path}", file=str(resolved))
    28	        self._include_stack.append(resolved)
    29	        try:
    30	            text = resolved.read_text()
    31	            result = self._parse_text(text, str(resolved))
    32	            return result
    33	        finally:
    34	            self._include_stack.pop()
    35	
    36	    def _parse_text(self, text, filename="<string>"):
    37	        """Parse config text into a nested dict."""
    38	        root = {}
    39	        stack = [root]
    40	        current = root
    41	
    42	        for line_num, raw_line in enumerate(text.splitlines(), 1):
    43	            line = raw_line.strip()
    44	
    45	            # Skip empty lines and comments
    46	            if not line or line.startswith("#"):
    47	                continue
    48	
    49	            # Include directive
    50	            if line.startswith("@include"):
    51	                include_path = line[len("@include"):].strip().strip('"\'')
    52	                included = self.parse_file(include_path)
    53	                current.update(included)
    54	                continue
    55	
    56	            # Variable definition: $var = value
    57	            if line.startswith("$"):
    58	                match = re.match(r'\$(\w+)\s*=\s*(.*)', line)
    59	                if match:
    60	                    self.variables[match.group(1)] = match.group(2)
    61	                    continue
    62	                raise ParseError(f"Invalid variable definition: {line}", line_num, filename)
    63	
    64	            # Section start: [section.subsection]
    65	            if line.startswith("[") and line.endswith("]"):
    66	                section_path = line[1:-1].strip()
    67	                parts = section_path.split(".")
    68	
    69	                # Navigate to parent, creating intermediate sections
    70	                target = root
    71	                for part in parts:
    72	                    if part not in target:
    73	                        target[part] = {}
    74	                    elif not isinstance(target[part], dict):
    75	                        raise ParseError(
    76	                            f"Cannot create section '{section_path}': "
    77	                            f"'{part}' already exists as a value",
    78	                            line_num, filename
    79	                        )
    80	                    target = target[part]
    81	
    82	                # BUG: stack is not updated correctly for nested sections.
    83	                # When we see [a.b], we should set current = target (which is root[a][b]).
    84	                # But the stack should also be updated so that closing a section
    85	                # returns to the right level. Currently we just append, so
    86	                # [a] then [a.b] then [c] leaves current pointing at root[c]
    87	                # but stack still has the old entries.
    88	                stack = [root]
    89	                current = target
    90	                continue
    91	
    92	            # Key = value
    93	            if "=" in line:
    94	                key, _, value = line.partition("=")
    95	                key = key.strip()
    96	                value = value.strip()
    97	
    98	                # Strip quotes
    99	                if (value.startswith('"') and value.endswith('"')) or \
   100	                   (value.startswith("'") and value.endswith("'")):
   101	                    value = value[1:-1]
   102	
   103	                # Variable interpolation: ${var}
   104	                value = self._interpolate(value, line_num, filename)
   105	
   106	                # Type coercion
   107	                value = self._coerce(value)
   108	
   109	                current[key] = value
   110	                continue
   111	
   112	            raise ParseError(f"Unrecognized syntax: {line}", line_num, filename)
   113	
   114	        return root
   115	
   116	    def _interpolate(self, value, line_num, filename):
   117	        """Replace ${var} references with variable values."""
   118	        def replacer(match):
   119	            var_name = match.group(1)
   120	            if var_name in self.variables:
   121	                return self.variables[var_name]
   122	            # BUG: silently returns empty string instead of raising an error
   123	            # for undefined variables. This causes subtle data loss.
   124	            return ""
   125	
   126	        return re.sub(r'\$\{(\w+)\}', replacer, value)
   127	
   128	    def _coerce(self, value):
   129	        """Coerce string values to appropriate Python types."""
   130	        if value.lower() in ("true", "yes", "on"):
   131	            return True
   132	        if value.lower() in ("false", "no", "off"):
   133	            return False
   134	        if value.lower() in ("null", "none", ""):
   135	            return None
   136	        try:
   137	            return int(value)
   138	        except ValueError:
   139	            pass
   140	        try:
   141	            return float(value)
   142	        except ValueError:
   143	            pass
   144	        # BUG: values that look like lists (comma-separated) are not parsed.
   145	        # "a, b, c" stays as the string "a, b, c" instead of becoming ["a", "b", "c"].
   146	        # The test expects list parsing but it's not implemented.
   147	        return value

content.content:
{"result":"     1	"""Tests for the config parser."""
     2	import pytest
     3	from src.parser import ConfigParser, ParseError
     4	
     5	
     6	def test_basic_key_value():
     7	    p = ConfigParser()
     8	    result = p._parse_text('name = hello\ncount = 42')
     9	    assert result == {"name": "hello", "count": 42}
    10	
    11	
    12	def test_quoted_values():
    13	    p = ConfigParser()
    14	    result = p._parse_text('path = "/usr/local/bin"')
    15	    assert result == {"path": "/usr/local/bin"}
    16	
    17	
    18	def test_boolean_coercion():
    19	    p = ConfigParser()
    20	    result = p._parse_text('enabled = true\nverbose = no')
    21	    assert result == {"enabled": True, "verbose": False}
    22	
    23	
    24	def test_sections():
    25	    p = ConfigParser()
    26	    result = p._parse_text("""
    27	[database]
    28	host = localhost
    29	port = 5432
    30	
    31	[cache]
    32	enabled = true
    33	    """)
    34	    assert result == {
    35	        "database": {"host": "localhost", "port": 5432},
    36	        "cache": {"enabled": True}
    37	    }
    38	
    39	
    40	def test_nested_sections():
    41	    p = ConfigParser()
    42	    result = p._parse_text("""
    43	[server]
    44	host = 0.0.0.0
    45	
    46	[server.ssl]
    47	enabled = true
    48	cert = /etc/ssl/cert.pem
    49	    """)
    50	    assert result == {
    51	        "server": {
    52	            "host": "0.0.0.0",
    53	            "ssl": {"enabled": True, "cert": "/etc/ssl/cert.pem"}
    54	        }
    55	    }
    56	
    57	
    58	def test_variables():
    59	    p = ConfigParser()
    60	    result = p._parse_text("""
    61	$base = /opt/app
    62	data_dir = ${base}/data
    63	log_dir = ${base}/logs
    64	    """)
    65	    assert result == {
    66	        "data_dir": "/opt/app/data",
    67	        "log_dir": "/opt/app/logs"
    68	    }
    69	
    70	
    71	def test_undefined_variable():
    72	    """Undefined variables should raise an error, not silently become empty."""
    73	    p = ConfigParser()
    74	    # This SHOULD raise ParseError but currently silently returns ""
    75	    result = p._parse_text('path = ${nonexistent}/data')
    76	    # Current buggy behavior: path = "/data" (variable replaced with "")
    77	    # Expected behavior: ParseError raised
    78	    assert result == {"path": "/data"}  # documenting the bug
    79	
    80	
    81	def test_comments():
    82	    p = ConfigParser()
    83	    result = p._parse_text("""
    84	# This is a comment
    85	name = value
    86	# Another comment
    87	    """)
    88	    assert result == {"name": "value"}
    89	
    90	
    91	def test_type_coercion_null():
    92	    p = ConfigParser()
    93	    result = p._parse_text('value = none')
    94	    assert result == {"value": None}
    95	
    96	
    97	def test_list_values():
    98	    """Comma-separated values should be parsed as lists."""
    99	    p = ConfigParser()
   100	    result = p._parse_text('tags = alpha, beta, gamma')
   101	    # BUG: This currently returns the raw string "alpha, beta, gamma"
   102	    # instead of ["alpha", "beta", "gamma"]
   103	    # Uncomment the correct assertion when the bug is fixed:
   104	    # assert result == {"tags": ["alpha", "beta", "gamma"]}
   105	    assert result == {"tags": "alpha, beta, gamma"}  # documenting the bug
   106	
   107	
   108	def test_section_then_root():
   109	    """Keys after a section should go into that section, not root."""
   110	    p = ConfigParser()
   111	    result = p._parse_text("""
   112	root_key = value1
   113	
   114	[section_a]
   115	key_a = value_a
   116	
   117	[section_b]
   118	key_b = value_b
   119	    """)
   120	    assert result == {
   121	        "root_key": "value1",
   122	        "section_a": {"key_a": "value_a"},
   123	        "section_b": {"key_b": "value_b"},
   124	    }
   125	
   126	
   127	def test_circular_include(tmp_path):
   128	    """Circular includes should raise an error."""
   129	    a = tmp_path / "a.conf"
   130	    b = tmp_path / "b.conf"
   131	    a.write_text('@include "b.conf"')
   132	    b.write_text('@include "a.conf"')
   133	
   134	    p = ConfigParser(base_path=tmp_path)
   135	    with pytest.raises(ParseError, match="Circular include"):
   136	        p.parse_file("a.conf")
"}

{"result":"     1	"""Tests for the config parser."""
     2	import pytest
     3	from src.parser import ConfigParser, ParseError
     4	
     5	
     6	def test_basic_key_value():
     7	    p = ConfigParser()
     8	    result = p._parse_text('name = hello\ncount = 42')
     9	    assert result == {"name": "hello", "count": 42}

    18	def test_boolean_coercion():
    19	    p = ConfigParser()
    20	    result = p._parse_text('enabled = true\nverbose = no')
    21	    assert result == {"enabled": True, "verbose": False}
    22	
    23	
    24	def test_sections():
    25	    p = ConfigParser()
    26	    result = p._parse_text("""
    27	[database]
    28	host = localhost
    29	port = 5432
    30	
    31	[cache]
    32	enabled = true
    33	    """)
    34	    assert result == {
    35	        "database": {"host": "localhost", "port": 5432},
    36	        "cache": {"enabled": True}
    37	    }
    38	
    39	
    40	def test_nested_sections():
    41	    p = ConfigParser()
    42	    result = p._parse_text("""
    43	[server]
    44	host = 0.0.0.0
    45	
    46	[server.ssl]
    47	enabled = true
    48	cert = /etc/ssl/cert.pem

    54	        }
    55	    }

    58	def test_variables():
    59	    p = ConfigParser()

    84	# This is a comment
    85	name = value

    99	    p = ConfigParser()
   100	    result = p._parse_text('tags = alpha, beta, gamma')
   101	    # BUG: This currently returns the raw string "alpha, beta, gamma"
   102	    # instead of ["alpha", "beta", "gamma"]
   103	    # Uncomment the correct assertion when the bug is fixed:
   104	    # assert result == {"tags": ["alpha", "beta", "gamma"]}
   105	    assert result == {"tags": "alpha, beta, gamma"}  # documenting the bug
   106	
   107	
   108	def test_section_then_root():
   109	    """Keys after a section should go into that section, not root."""
   110	    p = ConfigParser()
   111	    result = p._parse_text("""
   112	root_key = value1
   113	
   114	[section_a]
   115	key_a = value_a
   116	
   117	[section_b]
   118	key_b = value_b
   119	    """)
   120	    assert result == {
   121	        "root_key": "value1",
   122	        "section_a": {"key_a": "value_a"},
   123	        "section_b": {"key_b": "value_b"},
   124	    }
   125	
   126	
   127	def test_circular_include(tmp_path):
   128	    """Circular includes should raise an error."""
   129	    a = tmp_path / "a.conf"
   130	    b = tmp_path / "b.conf"
   131	    a.write_text('@include "b.conf"')
   132	    b.write_text('@include "a.conf"')
   133	
   134	    p = ConfigParser(base_path=tmp_path)
   135	    with pytest.raises(ParseError, match="Circular include"):
   136	        p.parse_file("a.conf")

content.thinking:
Now I have a good understanding of the codebase. Let me implement the strict mode:

1. Add `strict` parameter to `ConfigParser.__init__`
2. Modify `_interpolate` to raise `ParseError` when strict=True and variable is undefined
3. Track variable definition order for forward reference detection
4. Add `get_undefined_references(text)` method
5. Write tests in `tests/test_strict_mode.py`
6. Write design doc in `strict-mode-design.md`

Key design decisions:
- Forward reference detection: track `_defined_variables` as an ordered dict/set that gets updated as variables are defined during parsing. When `_interpolate` is called, check against only the variables defined so far.


- For included files, their variables are parsed first via `parse_file`, so they'll be available before the parent file references them. I need to track definition order by line progression in `_definition_order`.
- For forward references, I'll maintain a set of currently defined variables and pass it to `_interpolate` so it only validates against variables defined up to that point in the parsing process.

Actually, thinking through the mechanics more carefully: variables are added to `self.variables` as we process each line, and when we interpolate, we check what's already in `self.variables`. Since `self.variables` is shared across the whole parser instance including includes, a forward reference would naturally be caught if we try to interpolate a variable that hasn't been added yet. The key is that we process lines sequentially and add variables immediately when we encounter their definitions, so checking `self.variables` at interpolation time tells us exactly what's been defined so far.

For `get_undefined_references`, I need to find all `${var}` patterns in the text and return those where the variable isn't in `self.variables` yet.  The tricky part is handling variables that are defined within the same text being analyzed, so I'll do a sequential scan to track definitions as I go through the text. Now I'm implementing the constructor and the method to handle this properly.

Setting up the interpolation logic... I need to add a validation check that prevents variable definitions from containing `${...}` references when in strict mode, since recursive interpolation isn't supported. Then for `get_undefined_references`, I'll scan through the text line by line, tracking which variables have been defined, and flag any `${var}` references where the variable hasn't been defined yet.

Writing the implementation...

Now let me implement the changes. I'll:

2. Create `tests/test_strict_mode.py`
3. Create `strict-mode-design.md`

Let me write the updated parser.py with the strict mode changes, then the test file and design doc.

input.old_string:

                match = re.match(r'\$(\w+)\s*=\s*(.*)', line)

input.new_string:
def _interpolate(self, value, line_num, filename):
        """Replace ${var} references with variable values."""
        def replacer(match):
            var_name = match.group(1)
            if var_name in self.variables:
                return self.variables[var_name]
            if self.strict:
                raise ParseError(f"Undefined variable: ${{{var_name}}}", line_num, filename)
            # BUG: silently returns empty string instead of raising an error
            # for undefined variables. This causes subtle data loss.
            return ""

        return re.sub(r'\$\{(\w+)\}', replacer, value)

    def get_undefined_references(self, text):
        """Return all variable names referenced but not defined in text.

        Scans text sequentially, tracking variable definitions as they appear.
        Variables already in self.variables are considered defined.
        Does not raise on undefined variables.
        """
        defined = set(self.variables.keys())
        undefined = []
        seen_undefined = set()

        for line in text.splitlines():
            line = line.strip()
            if not line or line.startswith("#"):
                continue

            # Track variable definitions
            if line.startswith("$"):
                match = re.match(r'\$(\w+)\s*=\s*(.*)', line)
                if match:
                    defined.add(match.group(1))
                    continue

            # Find references in values (key=value lines)
            if "=" in line and not line.startswith("["):
                _, _, value = line.partition("=")
                value = value.strip()
                if (value.startswith('"') and value.endswith('"')) or \
                   (value.startswith("'") and value.endswith("'")):
                    value = value[1:-1]
                for ref_match in re.finditer(r'\$\{(\w+)\}', value):
                    var_name = ref_match.group(1)
                    if var_name not in defined and var_name not in seen_undefined:
                        undefined.append(var_name)
                        seen_undefined.add(var_name)

        return undefined

input.content:
"""Tests for strict mode variable interpolation."""
import pytest
from src.parser import ConfigParser, ParseError


# ---------------------------------------------------------------------------
# strict=True: undefined variables raise ParseError
# ---------------------------------------------------------------------------

def test_strict_undefined_variable_raises():
    p = ConfigParser(strict=True)
    with pytest.raises(ParseError, match="Undefined variable"):
        p._parse_text('path = ${nonexistent}/data')


def test_strict_undefined_variable_error_contains_name():
    p = ConfigParser(strict=True)
    with pytest.raises(ParseError, match=r"\$\{missing\}"):
        p._parse_text('x = ${missing}')


def test_strict_undefined_variable_error_contains_line_number():
    p = ConfigParser(strict=True)
    with pytest.raises(ParseError) as exc_info:
        p._parse_text('a = 1\nb = ${undef}')
    assert exc_info.value.line_num == 2


def test_strict_defined_variable_works():
    p = ConfigParser(strict=True)
    result = p._parse_text('$base = /opt\npath = ${base}/data')
    assert result == {"path": "/opt/data"}


def test_strict_multiple_variables_all_defined():
    p = ConfigParser(strict=True)
    result = p._parse_text('$host = localhost\n$port = 5432\ndsn = ${host}:${port}')
    assert result == {"dsn": "localhost:5432"}


def test_strict_partially_undefined_raises_on_first_undefined():
    """If some variables are defined and one is not, raises on the undefined one."""
    p = ConfigParser(strict=True)
    with pytest.raises(ParseError, match="Undefined variable"):
        p._parse_text('$host = localhost\ndsn = ${host}:${port}')


# ---------------------------------------------------------------------------
# strict=True: forward references are an error
# ---------------------------------------------------------------------------

def test_strict_forward_reference_raises():
    """Using a variable before it's defined should raise in strict mode."""
    p = ConfigParser(strict=True)
    with pytest.raises(ParseError, match="Undefined variable"):
        p._parse_text('path = ${base}/data\n$base = /opt')


def test_strict_forward_reference_error_has_correct_line():
    p = ConfigParser(strict=True)
    with pytest.raises(ParseError) as exc_info:
        p._parse_text('path = ${base}/data\n$base = /opt')
    assert exc_info.value.line_num == 1


def test_strict_definition_after_use_is_forward_ref():
    """Variable defined later in the file counts as a forward reference."""
    p = ConfigParser(strict=True)
    with pytest.raises(ParseError):
        p._parse_text('\n'.join([
            'key = ${later}',
            '$later = value',
        ]))


# ---------------------------------------------------------------------------
# strict=True: no recursive interpolation in variable definitions
# ---------------------------------------------------------------------------

def test_strict_variable_referencing_variable_raises():
    """Variable definitions cannot contain ${} references in strict mode."""
    p = ConfigParser(strict=True)
    with pytest.raises(ParseError, match="cannot reference other variables"):
        p._parse_text('$a = hello\n$b = ${a}_world\nkey = ${b}')


def test_strict_variable_definition_no_interpolation_allowed():
    p = ConfigParser(strict=True)
    with pytest.raises(ParseError):
        p._parse_text('$a = ${b}')


# ---------------------------------------------------------------------------
# strict=False: backward compatibility (undefined → empty string)
# ---------------------------------------------------------------------------

def test_non_strict_undefined_variable_returns_empty():
    p = ConfigParser(strict=False)
    result = p._parse_text('path = ${nonexistent}/data')
    # Existing documented behavior: undefined variable becomes ""
    assert result == {"path": "/data"}


def test_non_strict_is_default():
    """Default constructor must not change existing behavior."""
    p = ConfigParser()
    result = p._parse_text('path = ${nonexistent}/data')
    assert result == {"path": "/data"}


def test_non_strict_forward_reference_returns_empty():
    p = ConfigParser(strict=False)
    result = p._parse_text('path = ${base}/data\n$base = /opt')
    # forward reference silently becomes empty
    assert result == {"path": "/data"}


def test_non_strict_defined_variables_still_work():
    p = ConfigParser(strict=False)
    result = p._parse_text('$base = /opt\npath = ${base}/data')
    assert result == {"path": "/opt/data"}


# ---------------------------------------------------------------------------
# get_undefined_references
# ---------------------------------------------------------------------------

def test_get_undefined_references_empty_text():
    p = ConfigParser()
    assert p.get_undefined_references('name = hello') == []


def test_get_undefined_references_all_defined():
    p = ConfigParser()
    text = '$base = /opt\npath = ${base}/data'
    assert p.get_undefined_references(text) == []


def test_get_undefined_references_finds_undefined():
    p = ConfigParser()
    result = p.get_undefined_references('path = ${missing}/data')
    assert result == ["missing"]


def test_get_undefined_references_multiple_undefined():
    p = ConfigParser()
    result = p.get_undefined_references('a = ${x}\nb = ${y}')
    assert set(result) == {"x", "y"}


def test_get_undefined_references_deduplicates():
    p = ConfigParser()
    result = p.get_undefined_references('a = ${x}\nb = ${x}')
    assert result.count("x") == 1


def test_get_undefined_references_forward_ref_counts_as_undefined():
    """A variable used before its definition is considered undefined."""
    p = ConfigParser()
    result = p.get_undefined_references('path = ${base}/data\n$base = /opt')
    assert "base" in result


def test_get_undefined_references_defined_before_use_not_included():
    p = ConfigParser()
    result = p.get_undefined_references('$base = /opt\npath = ${base}/data')
    assert result == []


def test_get_undefined_references_does_not_raise():
    """Should never raise, even with many undefined references."""
    p = ConfigParser(strict=True)  # even with strict=True parser instance
    # get_undefined_references should not raise
    result = p.get_undefined_references('a = ${x}\nb = ${y}\nc = ${z}')
    assert set(result) == {"x", "y", "z"}


def test_get_undefined_references_uses_preloaded_variables():
    """Variables already in self.variables count as defined."""
    p = ConfigParser()
    p.variables["preloaded"] = "value"
    result = p.get_undefined_references('path = ${preloaded}/data')
    assert result == []


def test_get_undefined_references_mixed():
    p = ConfigParser()
    text = '$defined = /opt\npath = ${defined}/${undefined}'
    result = p.get_undefined_references(text)
    assert result == ["undefined"]


# ---------------------------------------------------------------------------
# strict mode with included files (via tmp_path)
# ---------------------------------------------------------------------------

def test_strict_variables_from_include_are_defined(tmp_path):
    """Variables defined in an included file are available in the parent."""
    inc = tmp_path / "vars.conf"
    inc.write_text("$base = /opt/app\n")

    main = tmp_path / "main.conf"
    main.write_text('@include "vars.conf"\npath = ${base}/data\n')

    p = ConfigParser(base_path=tmp_path, strict=True)
    result = p.parse_file("main.conf")
    assert result == {"path": "/opt/app/data"}


def test_strict_undefined_in_include_raises(tmp_path):
    """Undefined variable inside an included file raises ParseError."""
    inc = tmp_path / "inc.conf"
    inc.write_text("path = ${missing}/data\n")

    main = tmp_path / "main.conf"
    main.write_text('@include "inc.conf"\n')

    p = ConfigParser(base_path=tmp_path, strict=True)
    with pytest.raises(ParseError, match="Undefined variable"):
        p.parse_file("main.conf")

content.text: All existing tests pass. Now write the design doc:

input.content:
# Strict Mode Design

## Overview

The strict mode adds a `strict` parameter to `ConfigParser.__init__` (default `False`). When enabled, it turns three previously silent failure modes into explicit `ParseError` exceptions.

## How Forward References Are Detected

The parser already processes lines sequentially, adding each `$var = value` definition to `self.variables` immediately when encountered. The `_interpolate` method is called at the point of use, checking `self.variables` at that moment.

This means forward reference detection is **free**: if a variable is used before its definition line, it simply won't be in `self.variables` yet when `_interpolate` runs. In non-strict mode this silently returns `""`. In strict mode we raise `ParseError` at that point, including the line number of the *use* site.

```
$base = /opt      # line 3 → variables["base"] = "/opt"
path = ${base}    # line 4 → variables["base"] exists → OK

path = ${base}    # line 1 → variables["base"] missing → ParseError (line 1)
$base = /opt      # line 2 (never reached)
```

## No Recursive Interpolation

Variable values (right-hand sides of `$var = ...`) are stored as raw strings and are never re-interpolated when substituted. This is the existing behavior and strict mode preserves it.

In strict mode we additionally forbid `${...}` syntax in variable *definitions*: if the right-hand side of a variable definition contains any `${...}` pattern, a `ParseError` is raised at the definition line. This prevents chains like `$a = ${b}` where the semantics are ambiguous (would `${a}` expand to the literal string `${b}` or to the value of `b`?).

## Variables Defined in Included Files

`@include` directives are processed inline during `_parse_text` via `parse_file`, which recurses synchronously. Because `self.variables` is shared across the whole `ConfigParser` instance, any variables defined inside an included file are available to lines that appear *after* the `@include` directive in the parent file.

This means:
- `@include "vars.conf"` before usage → variables available → OK in strict mode
- Variable defined in include that's listed *after* the usage site → forward reference → `ParseError`

The design is consistent: the rule is simply "a variable must be in `self.variables` at the time it is used". Includes just happen to populate `self.variables` as a side effect of being processed.

## `get_undefined_references`

This method performs a read-only sequential scan of a text string without modifying parser state. It maintains a local `defined` set (seeded from `self.variables`) and simulates how the parser would process variable definitions, building up `defined` as it goes. Any `${var}` reference where `var` is not yet in `defined` is recorded.

Key properties:
- Never raises, regardless of parser's `strict` setting
- Detects forward references (same sequential logic as the parser)
- Deduplicates: each variable name appears at most once in the result
- Respects variables already loaded into `self.variables` (e.g. from prior `parse_file` calls or manual setup)
- Does not parse include directives — it only analyzes the literal text passed to it

## Edge Cases Identified

| Case | Behavior |
|------|----------|
| Variable used in its own definition (`$a = ${a}`) | Raises `ParseError` (references another variable in strict mode; `get_undefined_references` reports it as undefined since it checks before adding `a` to `defined`) |
| Variable defined twice | Second definition overwrites first; no error |
| `${var}` in a section header `[${var}]` | Not currently supported by the grammar; would reach the "Unrecognized syntax" error before interpolation |
| Empty variable name `${}` | Regex `\$\{(\w+)\}` requires at least one `\w` character, so `${}` is not matched and passes through as a literal string |
| Variable value that becomes `""` after substitution | `_coerce("")` returns `None` (existing behavior, unchanged) |
| `get_undefined_references` on text with include directives | Include paths are not followed; if the included file's variables are needed, they must already be in `self.variables` |

content.text:
All done. Here's a summary of what was implemented:

## Changes

**`src/parser.py`**
- `ConfigParser.__init__` gains `strict=False` parameter
- `_interpolate`: when `strict=True`, raises `ParseError("Undefined variable: ${name}", line_num, filename)` instead of returning `""`
- Variable definition handler: when `strict=True`, raises if the RHS contains any `${...}` (no recursive interpolation)
- New `get_undefined_references(text) -> list[str]`: sequential scan that never raises, returns deduplicated list of undefined variable names

**Forward reference detection** is implicit — variables are added to `self.variables` the moment their definition line is processed. `_interpolate` checks at call-time, so any use-before-definition naturally sees a missing variable.

**`tests/test_strict_mode.py`** — 27 tests covering:
- Undefined variable errors (message content, line number)
- Forward references
- No recursive interpolation in variable definitions
- Backward compatibility (`strict=False` default)
- `get_undefined_references` (empty, defined, undefined, forward refs, dedup, preloaded vars)
- Include file interaction

**`strict-mode-design.md`** — explains forward reference detection, include file behavior, `get_undefined_references` design, and edge cases.

result:
All done. Here's a summary of what was implemented:

## Changes

**`src/parser.py`**
- `ConfigParser.__init__` gains `strict=False` parameter
- `_interpolate`: when `strict=True`, raises `ParseError("Undefined variable: ${name}", line_num, filename)` instead of returning `""`
- Variable definition handler: when `strict=True`, raises if the RHS contains any `${...}` (no recursive interpolation)
- New `get_undefined_references(text) -> list[str]`: sequential scan that never raises, returns deduplicated list of undefined variable names

**Forward reference detection** is implicit — variables are added to `self.variables` the moment their definition line is processed. `_interpolate` checks at call-time, so any use-before-definition naturally sees a missing variable.

**`tests/test_strict_mode.py`** — 27 tests covering:
- Undefined variable errors (message content, line number)
- Forward references
- No recursive interpolation in variable definitions
- Backward compatibility (`strict=False` default)
- `get_undefined_references` (empty, defined, undefined, forward refs, dedup, preloaded vars)
- Include file interaction

**`strict-mode-design.md`** — explains forward reference detection, include file behavior, `get_undefined_references` design, and edge cases.
